blocks.body: Sentence Transformations — Relative Clauses / Linking Words shows how to join, reduce, or reshape parts of a sentence through relative clauses and linkers.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In these transformations, you are not rewriting one word but the relationship between two parts of the sentence: you add a relative clause, reduce one, or swap the linker without changing the logic.","rules":[{"label":"JOIN OR REDUCE","color":"emerald","text":"Sometimes you need to join two simple sentences into one, and sometimes you need to make the structure shorter through a different pattern.","example":"The woman who lives next door..."},{"label":"KEEP THE RELATION","color":"blue","text":"Check which relation must stay the same: reason, result, contrast, purpose, place, person, thing.","example":"although / because of / where / which"},{"label":"WATCH THE FORM AFTER LINKERS","color":"amber","text":"Some linkers need a noun phrase, while others need a full clause. That is where many errors appear.","example":"despite being tired / although he was tired"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. How to Spot the Needed Rewrite
blocks.body:
Start with the logical link between the ideas.

  • If the two parts describe the same person, thing, or place, you often need a relative clause: who, which, that, where.
  • If the task needs clause -> phrase, look for pairs such as although -> in spite of or because -> because of.
  • If the meaning is purpose, reason, or result, keep exactly that type of link.
  • In exam transformations, the real test is whether you can change the grammar without changing the logic.

blocks.heading: 2. Core Transformation Patterns
blocks.body:
Base sentenceTarget sentenceRule focus
The woman lives next door. She teaches chemistry.The woman who lives next door teaches chemistry.joining with a relative clause.
Although he was tired, he finished the report.In spite of being tired, he finished the report.although + clause -> in spite of + -ing.
The cafe is near the station. We met there.The cafe where we met is near the station.place reference -> where.
She went to bed early because she had an exam.She went to bed early because of her exam.because + clause -> because of + noun phrase.

If the keyword narrows your choice, make sure the new linker is followed by the right structure: a full clause, a noun phrase, or a gerund.

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Keeping a full clause after a linker that needs a noun phrase.","wrong":"Despite he was tired, he kept working.","right":"Despite being tired, he kept working. / Although he was tired, he kept working.","hint":"Despite / in spite of do not normally take a full clause by themselves."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Mixing up who, which, and where.","wrong":"The office who we visited was closed.","right":"The office which we visited was closed.","hint":"Choose the marker according to whether the noun is a person, a thing, or a place."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Changing the logical relation during the rewrite.","wrong":"He was ill, so he went to work. -> Although he was ill, he went to work.","right":"Choose a linker that keeps the same relation.","hint":"In transformation tasks, you cannot turn reason into contrast or result into purpose."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Forgetting the required form after the new linker.","wrong":"Because of he was late, we started without him.","right":"Because he was late, we started without him. / Because of his delay, we started without him.","hint":"After because of, you need a noun phrase, not a full clause."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick Exam Check","items":["Identify the logical relation first: person, thing, place, reason, result, contrast, purpose.","Check whether the new linker needs a clause, noun phrase, or -ing form.","In relative clauses, choose the right marker: who, which, that, where.","After rewriting, read both parts as one message: does the logic still stay exactly the same?"]}
